unitTitle: KS2 Y4 The internet (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Connecting networks
lessons.objectives:
I can describe the internet as a network of networks
I can show how information is shared across the internet
I can name a router and say what it does
I can say why a network needs protecting
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: internet · network · router · network security. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; string/rope ready for the role-play. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what is the internet? Recap from Year 3: a network is computers joined together; a switch passes the message to the right computer. Pupils CHOOSE that the internet is lots of networks joined together (a network of networks), shown with the world cable map.
   • Support: true/not-true about networks and the internet.  • Core: say what the internet is in their own words.  • Challenge: “a network of networks” describes the internet.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — the router. A switch passes a message INSIDE one network; a router passes a message to ANOTHER network. Likely error: mixing up switch and router → fix-words “the Router Reaches Another network.”
3. (20–35) You-do — order the message journey across two networks. Pupils drag computer → switch → router → switch → computer into order (the `order` task) on the activity worksheet.
4. (35–48) Network security + be the internet. A network is kept safe so only the right people get in (cohort-level, calm — like a locked door for the school; no personal online stories). Calm role-play: pass a message to another ‘network’ through the switch and router. Screenshot/photo for show-your-work. Challenge multi-select the true sentences.
5. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil says what a router does.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: What is the internet made of?
lessons.objectives:
I can name networked devices and say how they connect
I can say the internet gives us many services
I can recognise the World Wide Web is part of the internet
I can tell the difference between a website and a web page
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: switch · server · router · internet · World Wide Web · website · web page. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; a website ready to display. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — match the device to its job. Pupils match switch / server / router to what each does (matching grid) using the device images.
   • Support: which device joins one network to another.  • Core: why routers are needed to make the internet.  • Challenge: the internet is made of wires and routers.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — services. The internet is the wires, routers and devices; it carries SERVICES — the World Wide Web, files, email. Analogy: the internet is the roads, the services are the journeys.
3. (25–40) You-do — internet vs World Wide Web. Pupils SORT items into internet vs World Wide Web (the `sort` task). Key misconception to fix: the internet and the web are the same → fix-words “the web is one part of the internet, not all of it.”
4. (40–50) Website vs web page + explore. A website is a collection of pages; a web page is one page. Show a home page, then click to one page. Pupils explore one chosen website and screenshot it for show-your-work. Challenge multi-select the services the internet carries.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil names one service.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Sharing information
lessons.objectives:
I can say what types of media can be shared on the web
I can describe where websites are stored
I can describe how to get to a website
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: media · server · data centre · web browser. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; a browser open for everyone. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–12) Starter — can it be shared on the web? We share MEDIA: pictures, videos, text, sounds; we cannot share real physical things. Pupils SORT can-share vs cannot-share (the `sort` task).
   • Support: photo of a dog (true) vs real dog (not true).  • Core: why a photo of a sandwich shares but a real one doesn’t.  • Challenge: name the kinds of media.
2. (12–25) I-do — where websites are stored. A website is stored on a SERVER; many servers live in a DATA CENTRE; data centres are all around the world.
3. (25–40) I-do → we-do — how we get to a website. We use a WEB BROWSER (Chrome, Safari, Edge, Firefox) on a computer, tablet, phone or console. Misconception to fix: “you need Google to use the internet” → Google is a search engine; Chrome is the browser.
4. (40–50) You-do — open one website in a browser, find which browser it is, screenshot for show-your-work. Challenge multi-select the devices you can open a website on.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil says where a website is stored.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: What is a website?
lessons.objectives:
I can name the parts of a website
I can recognise that I can add content to the web
I can use a web tool to create content online
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: website · web page · content · logo · links · media. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the labelling image ready; Chrome Music Lab open. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–12) Starter — label the parts of a website. Pupils drag Logo / Search box / Menu links / Picture onto the website image (the `label` task).
   • Support: which part is the logo / the search box.  • Core: name two parts.  • Challenge: what links are for.
2. (12–25) I-do → we-do — people make content. Websites are made by people; on some sites anyone can ADD content (a blog, a comment, a project). Good: easy to share knowledge. Tricky: not everything added is true or kind (cohort-level; sets up lessons 5–6).
3. (25–45) You-do — make your own content. Pupils use Chrome Music Lab (Kandinsky) to create a sound/pattern and screenshot it for show-your-work. SEND note: Music Lab plays sound — keep volume low / offer headphones; no flashing visuals. Challenge multi-select the media found on websites.
4. (45–52) Share — “you added your own content to the web.” Core writes one good + one tricky thing about anyone adding content.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil names one part of a website.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Who owns the web?
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that websites and their content are made by people
I can suggest who owns the content on a website
I can explain that there are rules to protect content
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: content · owner · copyright · permission · credit. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; a website ready with a copyright notice. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — who made this content? All content is made by people: one person, a company, or a community. Pupils match a YouTuber (one person), LEGO (a company) and Scratch (a community) to who made it (matching grid).
   • Support: content is made by people.  • Core: name a website and who made it.  • Challenge: a big site is made by a company (a team).
2. (10–20) We-do — owning and sharing in the real world. Pupils SORT yours-to-keep (your money, your drawing) vs borrow-and-give-back (a library book, park equipment) (the `sort` task). Bridge: seeing something does not make it yours.
3. (20–40) I-do → you-do — rules protect content. Content belongs to the maker; COPYRIGHT is a rule that protects it; give the maker CREDIT. Fix-words for “it’s online so it’s free”: “you can SEE it, but it still belongs to the maker.” Pupils find a clue (a © notice / a name under a photo) on a chosen site and screenshot it for show-your-work. Challenge multi-select the true sentences.
4. (40–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil says what copyright protects.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Can I believe what I read?
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that not everything on the web is true
I can explain why some information online may not be honest or accurate
I can explain why I need to think before I share or reshare content
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: information · true · false · accurate · honest · share. Keep the whole lesson calm and cohort-level — no personal online stories. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — is this picture real? Show the budgie-with-a-tiger’s-head (an edited picture). Pupils CHOOSE real / edited. Links to the Year 4 photo-editing unit. Fix-words: “if it looks impossible, it may be edited.”
   • Support: some pictures are edited (true); everything is true (not true).  • Core: how can you tell it is not real.  • Challenge: it is sensible to check if something is true.
2. (10–25) I-do — why some information is not true: to make money, to be popular, by mistake. Keep it factual and brief.
3. (25–40) You-do — how information spreads. Pupils ORDER one person → friends → many people → cannot take it back (the `order` task). Big idea: sharing is fast and hard to undo.
4. (40–52) Think before you share. Checks: is it true, who made it, ask a trusted adult if unsure. Challenge multi-select the sensible checks. Pupils look at one thing online, decide if they believe it and why, and screenshot it for show-your-work.
5. (52–55) End of unit — self-tick the three “I can…”; recap the journey: networks → the internet → the World Wide Web → owning and trusting content.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
